3-ways-to-share-nodejs-modules-across-mu.author: trakkif Ltd
3-ways-to-share-nodejs-modules-across-mu.html: hi, thanks for tabling out these 3 options. The trouble is that all are quite 'clunky' - developers have to ensure they are pulling/updating with the latest changes at all times, not just automatically getting those updates. The least clunky is using npm publish, but this requires every slight change to increment the version number to ensure its seen as an update - which is ok in a very controlled environment but not when a lot of co-development is going on (imagine a repo that is being updated 20 ... 50 times a day). I did think npm link might be a good option here, but this doesn't appear to be a robust approach. I'm still stuck on a better option!

3-ways-to-share-nodejs-modules-across-mu.author: code
3-ways-to-share-nodejs-modules-across-mu.html:
Honestly by far the best option is just to use Bit: https://bit.dev

It's the fastest, simplest, and most scalable way to share NodeSJ code in a managed way. It's open source and free enjoy.

3-ways-to-share-nodejs-modules-across-mu.author: Elvis Van
3-ways-to-share-nodejs-modules-across-mu.html: how do you know that? can you describe in details how bitdev is better?

3-ways-to-share-nodejs-modules-across-mu.author: mitya33
3-ways-to-share-nodejs-modules-across-mu.html:
The obvious problem with option 3 is you may not (probably won't) want your shared library to be public if its reason for being is to service your own apps. npm link isn't an option for most cloud hosting since you wouldn't normally check in your node_modules folder (the cloud service builds that directory itself) and thus any "linked" modules wouldn't get pulled in.

Option 2 is the most viable and scalable, I'd say. The issue of developers having to repeatedly pull down changes to your library can be mitigated by building that step into the startup command e.g. by running the whole app in a Docker container.

ahoy-parse-ye-node-js-command-args-with-.author: Justin
ahoy-parse-ye-node-js-command-args-with-.html:
Nice example. 'url' is not a command though and it's listed as a command in the help output.

I'm looking for an example of yargs usage where there are multiple commands… and by default if no command is given, I want to fall back to --help output. Do you happen to know how to do this?

ahoy-parse-ye-node-js-command-args-with-.author: David Neal
ahoy-parse-ye-node-js-command-args-with-.html:
Hi Justin,

In this example, the url command is any text you might type in as the first argument. You can certainly have more than one. I think it's a good practice to have arbitrary input arguments first, then followed by any command-line switches.

There are a couple of ways you can trigger help output. One is to make at least one of the commands required. Another is to check the length of the arguments, and use yargs.showHelp(). Here's an example:

var yargs = require( "yargs" );
var options = yargs
.usage( "Usage: $0 <url> [-u \"username\"] [-p \"password\"] [--post] [--data \"{key:value}\"]" )
.command( "url", "URL to request", { alias: "url" } )
// .required( 1, "URL is required" )
.option( "u", { alias: "user", demand: false, describe: "Username", type: "string" } )
.option( "p", { alias: "password", demand: false, describe: "Password", type: "string" } )
.option( "d", { alias: "data", describe: "Data to send as JSON", type: "string" } )
.option( "get", { describe: "Use HTTP GET", type: "boolean" } )
.option( "post", { describe: "Use HTTP POST", type: "boolean" } )
.option( "put", { describe: "Use HTTP PUT", type: "boolean" } )
.option( "del", { describe: "Use HTTP DELETE", type: "boolean" } )
.help( "?" )
.alias( "?", "help" )
.example( "$0 https://example.com/api/posts", "Get a list of posts" )
.example( "$0 https://example.com/api/posts --post --data \"{ 'title': 'Avast ye!', 'body': 'Thar be a post hyar!'}\"", "Create a new post" )
.epilog( "Copyright 2015 ReverentGeek" )
.argv;

// Check to see if no args were passed
if ( options._.length === 0 ) {
yargs.showHelp();
} else {
// Get the URL from the first parameter
var url = options._[ 0 ];

console.log( "url:", url );

// Make "get" the default if no verb is specified
if ( !options.get && !options.post && !options.put && !options.del ) {
options.get = true;
}
}

console.log( "options:", options );

Hope this helps!

ahoy-parse-ye-node-js-command-args-with-.author: Justin
ahoy-parse-ye-node-js-command-args-with-.html:
Thanks David! Ye the simplest way seems to be:


  .required( 1, 'At least one command is required')

:)

ai-really-is-taking-my-job.author: RandomCake
ai-really-is-taking-my-job.html:
Great post, gen AI is so handy, but it's pretty concerning that we might stop actually creating new styles, with just re-hashed versions of what we've already done.

Sure, there will be new creative things done with the gen AI, but I don't think that's a great substitute for human creators who have new ideas.

ai-really-is-taking-my-job.author: undisclosed location
ai-really-is-taking-my-job.html:
We used to call rehashed art ideas "schools of art" such as the Hudson River Valley, Impressionist, Pointillist, etc.

I'm not concerned with rehashing versions of what we have already. Almost all commercial art is a rehash, a lot of new art is a rehash of what people have seen, poorly remembered, and some even deliberately try to obscure the origins.

I suggest examining why you feel rehashing is a problem.

Is it ego? Is it some loss of humanness? If you think it's something unique to humans, look over history and see just how many times humans have been knocked out of their special places in the universe over and over and over again to the point where now we finally understand that we're not the only sapient beings with tool use and language. We've just been at it longer on this planet.

Interested in hearing your thoughts on the matter!

hosting-node-js-on-microsoft-azure.author: Faran Iqbal
hosting-node-js-on-microsoft-azure.html: I have made a virtual machine with windows server R2 2012 on windows azure, I have configured mongodb and nodejs restful api on the virtual machine, but i don't know how to make my node app public.

hosting-node-js-on-microsoft-azure.author: David Neal
hosting-node-js-on-microsoft-azure.html: By default, all public ports are turned off on Azure virtual machines. You will need to open a public port in the Azure management portal for HTTP traffic. In the Azure management portal, click on "Virtual machines," click on your virtual machine, and then click on the "Endpoints" tab. From there you can add a public port 80 (HTTP) to point to the port your Node.js application is listening on (private port). Node.js can be listening on port 80, or any other port that is available. Hope this helps!

hosting-node-js-on-microsoft-azure.author: Quantumventura
hosting-node-js-on-microsoft-azure.html: Hello David, we are running node.js/mongoDb/ socket.io as a windows app. The app works fine on a local server but once published on azure, the app is unstable. Keep getting http status 500 and substatus 1013 errors. node.exe works fine but it looks like IIS is the culprit. Our app reads twitter realtime streams based on user defined search criteria. I have 2 questions. a) if we go for dedicated VM instead of running it as windows app, do we still need IIS or we can run without it? b) If IIS server is a must, what are the best debugging tools? someone suggested node inspector. thanks so much. Mike

hosting-node-js-on-microsoft-azure.author: David Neal
hosting-node-js-on-microsoft-azure.html: If you run a dedicated VM, you do not have to use IIS to host Node.js. You can use something like winser to manage an instance of your application as if it were a Windows service (start/stop/restart).

meet-surrealdb-nodejs.author: Rick Culpepper
meet-surrealdb-nodejs.html:
Holy guacamole, Batman!

After reading this post, I spent a couple of days investigating SurrealDB. They got the name right—surreal.

I haven’t used SurrealDB for anything serious yet, but the possibilities are staggering. It seems inevitable that someone will develop a single platform supporting SQL, NoSQL, and GraphQL. This is like the Swiss Army knife of database platforms.

However, SurrealDB faces challenges in the real world of software engineering and application performance.

First, SurrealDB’s complexity may overwhelm developers, especially those unfamiliar with SQL, NoSQL, and GraphQL. Engineers familiar with all three should focus on using SurrealDB effectively for each use case. Less experienced engineers might struggle to choose the right options, leading to confusion, lack of focus, and slow development. This doesn’t account for the initial learning curve to adopt SurrealDB.

Second, I wonder about SurrealDB’s performance at scale. Can it perform on par with existing SQL, NoSQL, and GraphQL platforms? Maybe. While a Swiss Army knife can address many tooling needs, it can be hard to find the right “blade” when needed. One or two tools might be more useful in most situations. “Do-it-all” tools like SurrealDB may sacrifice performance for flexibility. More specialized tools (e.g., MongoDB, SQL Server, PostgreSQL) typically provide top-notch performance for specific use cases.

Thanks for sharing!

sql-server-and-nodejs-revisited.author: Pierre Bourgault
sql-server-and-nodejs-revisited.html: Took a quick look at seriate, looks cool. I could not see support for Integrated Security (SSPI) though. Am I correct ?

sql-server-and-nodejs-revisited.author: David Neal
sql-server-and-nodejs-revisited.html:
Seriate supports trusted/NTLM authentication, which you can read about on the seriate repository at https://github.com/LeanKit-....

By SSPI, I'm assuming you mean it authenticates with the user context under which the node application is currently running? Seriate depends on the mssql module for the underlying SQL work. As far as I can tell, mssql does not support SSPI.

sql-server-and-nodejs-revisited.author: Pierre Bourgault
sql-server-and-nodejs-revisited.html: Thanks for the quick reply! Yes, that's what I meant. I can do SSPI authentication easily with pyodbc in python but fails to find something as easy for node.js.

sql-server-for-linux-how-to-move-a-datab.author: lporras16
sql-server-for-linux-how-to-move-a-datab.html:
Hey I'm using this repository to use sql server on docker: https://hub.docker.com/r/mi... but it does not include the command sqlpackage on /opt/mssql/bin/, it has:

root@7fb9d1dbbf00:/opt/mssql-tools/bin# ls
bcp sqlcmd

How can I restore a database from a bacpac file if I don't have sqlpackage?

sql-server-for-linux-how-to-move-a-datab.author: David Neal
sql-server-for-linux-how-to-move-a-datab.html: Hey! Yeah, MS removed the sqlpackage tools at some point. You can use the BACKUP and RESTORE DATABASE sql commands to restore a backup (.bak) file created on a Windows Server. The same steps to copy a file into a Docker container still apply. This article might help: https://docs.microsoft.com/...

sql-server-for-linux-how-to-move-a-datab.author: lporras16
sql-server-for-linux-how-to-move-a-datab.html: thanks for your response. Do you know if I can use RESTORE DATABASE with a bacpac file? I don't have a .bak file, I'm using Azure to create the backup file, and it only returns a bacpac file. I tried to restore using the software sql Operations Studio, but it only opens .bak files :/

sql-server-running-on-a-mac.author: Vincent Congini
sql-server-running-on-a-mac.html: I didn't even think about scripting out the database, so thanks for that tip. I was curious if you've been able to perform a restore into the docker container successfully? I'm just scratching my head at it today.

sql-server-running-on-a-mac.author: anon
sql-server-running-on-a-mac.html:
1) get container ID: $docker inspect -f '{{.Id}}' my-mssql

2) copy BAK file to docker instance: docker exec -i <container_id> bash -c 'cat > /var/opt/mssql/backup.bak' < '/source/path/backup.bak'

3) log into mssql: mssql -u sa -p 'myPassword'

3) restore db: RESTORE DATABASE [MyDatabase] FROM DISK = N'/var/opt/mssql/backup.bak' WITH FILE = 1, NOUNLOAD, REPLACE, STATS = 5

sql-server-running-on-a-mac.author: Theara
sql-server-running-on-a-mac.html:
I run `docker run --name name_your_container -e 'ACCEPT_EULA=Y' -e 'SA_PASSWORD=123456' -p 1433:1433 -d microsoft/mssql-server-linux`.
It show
```
Microsoft(R) SQL Server(R) setup failed with error code 1. Please check the setup log in /var/opt/mssql/log for more information.
```
But I check the status it work fine (docker ps -a).
And then I run connect by `mssql -s localhost -u sa -p 123456 -o 1433`:

```
2017-01-20 13:14:56.46 Logon Error: 18456, Severity: 14, State: 7.
2017-01-20 13:14:56.46 Logon Login failed for user 'sa'. Reason: An error occurred while evaluating the password. [CLIENT: 172.17.0.1]
```

sql-server-running-on-a-mac.author: anon
sql-server-running-on-a-mac.html: I would get this error when my password did not meet microsoft's standards for a password, which I believe needs a special char, numbers, letters (lower and cap). you should create a new docker container with a valid password and you should be able to login no problem

sql-server-running-on-a-mac.author: David Neal
sql-server-running-on-a-mac.html:
Hi Stephen... I think it might help if you described the exact steps you performed up until the error. I'll try to reproduce or see where the issue may be. Maybe use screencast https://www.techsmith.com/s... to record the steps and error?

Best regards,
David

state-of-the-reverentgeek-june-2016.author: Pavneet Singh Saund
state-of-the-reverentgeek-june-2016.html:
David, it was great to get to meet you at NDC Oslo. First on Jeremy Clark's slides, then in person during the conference. It's also been a pleasure following you on twitter.
Haven't seen your Electron talk yet, but it's on my watch-playlist :).

Really inspiring to see how you created your avatar. Makes me want to invest in an iPad pro / pencil and start drawing myself.

Could have really used your upcoming talk on public speaking before I went on the stage at NDC Oslo. Sounds like an awesome topic.

Great tip on writing down what you're grateful for daily. I think Kylie Hunt also mentioned that in her talk. Something I've started doing actually.

Hope to bump in to you some time soon!

state-of-the-reverentgeek-june-2016.author: David Neal
state-of-the-reverentgeek-june-2016.html:
It was a pleasure to meet you! Thanks for the encouragement. My plan is to publish a blog post on using hand-drawn illustrations. Feedback from the public speaking idea has been very positive. I hope I can deliver!

Hope you have a great day!
